title: Abutilon pilosum
intro: Abutilon pilosum, also known as the hairy abutilon or hairy velvet flower, is a plant species in the Malvaceae family, native to tropical and subtropical regions. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin problems, due to its anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Abutilon pilosum include flavonoids, saponins, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The plant is commonly used to make herbal teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat various ailments, including fever, sore throats, and digestive issues.
taxonomy: Abutilon pilosum is classified within the family Malvaceae. This family includes a wide variety of plants commonly known as mallows or mallow family. Within the genus Abutilon, Abutilon pilosum is one of the many species, which are mainly native to tropical and subtropical regions. The Malvaceae family is part of the order Malvales and the class Magnoliopsida. Abutilon pilosum's classification helps in understanding its evolutionary relationships with other plants.
uses: Abutilon pilosum has been traditionally used in various cultures for its medicinal properties. In some regions, it is employed to treat fever, rheumatism, and other inflammatory conditions. The plant's leaves and roots are often used in herbal remedies, and its extracts are said to possess antibacterial and antifungal properties. Abutilon pilosum is also believed to have anti-inflammatory effects, which may help to soothe skin irritations and wounds. More research is needed to fully understand the plant's potential medicinal uses and benefits.

side_effects: Abutilon pilosum, like many plants in the Malvaceae family, may have potential side effects due to its toxicity. The plant contains saponins, which can cause gastrointestinal issues, skin irritation, and other adverse reactions when ingested or come into contact with the skin. Ingestion of the plant can also lead to nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. The sap of Abutilon pilosum may also cause allergic reactions in some individuals, resulting in skin redness, itching, and inflammation. Further research is needed to fully understand the potential side effects of Abutilon pilosum.
